title: All elementary functions from a single binary operator
author: Andrzej Odrzywołek

chunks.title_en: Positivity of the left exponential of eml
exp(x) as eml — corollary phrasing of 007
Additive consequence: x + y via exp and ln (specialized)
Negation realised in the Calc-3 set
An EML term whose eval is e
EML term with x-leaf whose eval is exp(x)
Minimality: three primitives is the minimum
EMLTerm whose eval is i (imaginary unit)
Master-formula parameter count at level n
Count of EMLTerms equals the Catalan number

chunks.prompt:
Prove this Lean theorem against Lean 4.28.0 + Mathlib v4.28.0.

Context: Applying the EML operator to two unit arguments yields Euler's number: eml(1,1) = exp(1) − ln(1) = e − 0 = e. The simplest of the fundamental examples.

import Mathlib.Analysis.SpecialFunctions.Exp
import Mathlib.Analysis.SpecialFunctions.Log.Basic

namespace EML

def eml (x y : ℝ) : ℝ := Real.exp x - Real.log y

theorem eml_

Prove this Lean theorem against Lean 4.28.0 + Mathlib v4.28.0.

Context: Setting the second argument to 1 collapses ln(1) to zero, so eml(x,1) = exp(x) for every real x. This shows that the exponential is immediately reachable in EML.

Prove this Lean theorem against Lean 4.28.0 + Mathlib v4.28.0.

Context: Setting the first argument to 1 reduces exp(1) to the constant e, giving eml(1,y) = e − ln(y). The hypothesis y > 0 is not formally required (Real.log is junk-valued elsewhere) but we keep it for semantic clarity.

Prove this Lean theorem against Lean 4.28.0 + Mathlib v4.28.0.

Context: The natural log expands as a triple-nested EML application: ln(z) = eml(1, eml(eml(1, z), 1)). The proof unfolds eml repeatedly and uses Real.log_one, Real.log_exp plus arithmetic. For z > 0 all the inner Real.log arguments are positive.

Prove this Lean theorem against Lean 4.28.0 + Mathlib v4.28.0.

Context: We show that negation −x is reachable using only addition and inversion (the Calc-3 operator set minus the standalone −x primitive): −x = 1/(1/(1/x+1)−1) + 1 for x ≠ 0, x ≠ −1. This is the step that removes −x as a primitive on the way to Calc 2/Calc 1.

import Mathlib.Tactic.FieldSimp

namespace EM

Prove this Lean theorem against Lean 4.28.0 + Mathlib v4.28.0.

Context: For every EML term, size t ≥ 1. Inductive proof: leaves have size 1; nodes have 1 + size t + size u ≥ 1.

namespace EML

inductive EMLTerm : Type
  | one : EMLTerm
  | eml : EMLTerm → EMLTerm → EMLTerm
  deriving Repr

def EMLTerm.size : EMLTerm → ℕ
  | .one => 1
  | .eml t u => 1 + EMLTerm.size t + EMLTerm.size u

theorem EM

Prove this Lean theorem against Lean 4.28.0 + Mathlib v4.28.0.

Context: The EML term eml(.one, .one) evaluates to exp(1) − ln(1) = e. A constructive witness that e lies in the image of eval. The term's size is 3 (one node + two leaves), matching the K column in the paper's catalogue.

Prove this Lean theorem against Lean 4.28.0 + Mathlib v4.28.0.

Context: To realise exp(x) as an EML term we add a .var leaf representing the variable x; the evaluation at x gives EMLTerm₁.eval x (.eml .var .one) = exp(x). We introduce a separate type EMLTerm₁ to avoid disturbing the original (constants-only) EMLTerm.
